narrative:
A federal judge has ruled against the Trump administration's policy of deporting immigrants to "third countries" — nations other than their home countries where they have no ties or citizenship [1][2][3]. U.S. District Judge Brian Murphy, appointed by former President Biden, issued an 81-page ruling on Wednesday declaring the Department of Homeland Security's third-country removal policy unconstitutional and a violation of due process protections [2][3].

## Key Ruling Details

Judge Murphy ruled that immigrants must receive "meaningful notice" before deportation to third countries and have the opportunity to challenge their removal, particularly if they fear persecution or torture [3]. The judge criticized the policy's lack of transparency, writing: "This new policy — which purports to stand in for the protections Congress has mandated — fails to satisfy due process for a raft of reasons, not least of which is that nobody really knows anything about these purported 'assurances'" [3].

The ruling requires that federal authorities attempt to remove immigrants to their home country or another country approved by an immigration judge, rather than arbitrary third countries [3]. However, the judge paused his ruling to allow for an appeal [1], and the government has 15 days to appeal the order [3].

## Policy Background and Implementation

The Trump administration has deported immigrants to countries including Eswatini, Rwanda, Ghana, El Salvador, and South Sudan as part of this strategy [3]. DHS officials have previously claimed "undisputed authority" to deport criminal immigrants to third countries that have agreed to accept them [2]. The policy targets what officials describe as the "worst of the worst" — individuals "so uniquely barbaric that their own countries won't take them back, including convicted murderers, child rapists and drug traffickers" [2].

## Previous Legal Challenges

This represents the latest development in ongoing litigation. The Supreme Court previously ruled in June against Judge Murphy's earlier order that had prevented third-country deportations, allowing the Trump administration to deport eight immigrants with criminal records to South Sudan, where none held citizenship [3]. The current ruling stems from a class-action lawsuit filed in Boston challenging DHS's deportation process [2].

## Political Context

Former Assistant Secretary Tricia McLaughlin defended the policy in June, arguing that without it, dangerous criminals would "walk free on American streets" [2]. She criticized the Biden administration for creating a "national security nightmare" through what she characterized as lax immigration enforcement that "allowed millions of illegal aliens to flood our country" [2]. The policy represents a key component of Trump's campaign promise of mass deportations to address immigration backlogs [2].

citations.title: Judge Finds Trump Administration's Third-Country Deportations Unlawful
citations.quote: The ruling repudiates a key Homeland Security Department policy of sending immigrants to countries where they have no ties. The judge paused his ruling to allow for an appeal.

citations.title: Biden-appointed federal judge rules Trump's 'third country' deportation policy is unconstitutional
citations.quote: A federal judge appointed by former President Joe Biden on Wednesday blocked the Trump administration from deporting illegal immigrants to so-called 'third countries' without first giving them notice and an opportunity to challenge their removal

citations.title: Biden-Appointed Judge Rules Against Trump's Third Country Deportations
citations.quote: Judge Brian E. Murphy of the Federal District Court for the District of Massachusetts ruled that the Trump administration can't deport illegal immigrants to third countries, which are nations other than their native soil.

keyDevelopments: Federal judge rules Trump's third-country deportation policy unconstitutional
Court requires 'meaningful notice' and opportunity to challenge deportations to third countries
Judge pauses ruling to allow government 15 days to appeal
Policy has resulted in deportations to countries including Rwanda, Ghana, El Salvador, and South Sudan
Supreme Court previously allowed policy to continue in June, enabling deportation of 8 individuals to South Sudan
